contact_information.what_parish_group_do_you_belong_to: St. Augustine Catholic Church, Coral Gables – Young Adults & Music Ministry
contact_information.how_did_you_hear_about_us:
I first heard about the Archdiocese of Miami Pilgrimage to Lourdes through a combination of parish announcements and personal recommendations. Our pastor at St. Augustine Catholic Church in Coral Gables mentioned it during the announcements at the end of Sunday Mass around early September, highlighting that this would be the 5th Annual pilgrimage and that it was especially focused on service to the sick and deepening Marian devotion. 

A week later, one of my close friends from the parish, Ana Lucia, who went on the pilgrimage two years ago as a volunteer, shared her experience in detail with our Young Adults group. She described accompanying sick pilgrims to the baths, participating in the candlelight processions, and praying the rosary at the Grotto. Hearing her describe how profoundly it impacted her faith and how organized and supportive the Archdiocese team was made me seriously consider applying.

I also saw a flyer posted on the bulletin board at the back of our church, listing the dates, approximate cost, and contact information for the Archdiocese of Miami Office of Pilgrimages. That prompted me to visit the Archdiocese website, where I found a dedicated page for the Lourdes pilgrimage with FAQs, testimonies from past participants, and a short video recap of last year’s trip. 

Finally, during a rehearsal with the music ministry, a couple of choir members mentioned that they were thinking of going this year, possibly as regular pilgrims, and encouraged me to look into it because of my interest in Marian shrines and service. All these combined—parish announcements, friend’s personal testimony, the bulletin flyer, the Archdiocese website, and the encouragement of my parish community—led me to this application.

travel_preferences.list_any_travel_companions:
I am planning to travel with one close friend from my parish, Ana Lucia Fernandez (age 35), who is also applying as a regular pilgrim. Ana and I currently serve together in the Young Adults ministry and occasionally help with hospitality after the Sunday evening Mass at St. Augustine. We have coordinated our vacation time from work so that we can be on the same pilgrimage dates (September 16–27). While we are each submitting our own application and understand that rooming and seating arrangements cannot be guaranteed, we are hoping, if possible, to be placed in the same small group or at least on the same bus for local transfers. 

Ana has shared that she previously traveled on this pilgrimage two years ago and is comfortable with the schedule and expectations, which reassures me as this will be my first time in Lourdes. We have already discussed practical details, such as sharing some toiletries to save luggage space, praying together daily during the pilgrimage, and supporting one another if either of us feels overwhelmed by the emotional aspects of serving the sick. 

If for any reason Ana’s application is not accepted or she cannot travel, I am still committed to going on the pilgrimage on my own. I am comfortable being placed with other pilgrims or volunteers and understand that companionship arrangements ultimately depend on the needs of the group and the organizers.

languages.other_languages_you_speak_fluently:
I am fully fluent in English and Spanish (read, write, and speak) and use both daily at home and at work. English is my primary language for professional communication, while Spanish is the main language I use with my parents and extended family, as well as many parishioners in our community.

I also have a very basic, conversational knowledge of Italian from taking two semesters in college and using language apps over the past few years. I can handle simple greetings, order food, and understand some basic phrases, but I would not consider myself fluent, and I would not be comfortable serving in an official capacity in Italian. I do not speak French beyond a few memorized phrases like “bonjour,” “merci,” and “s’il vous plaît.”

sanctuary_service_information.service_preference:
Although I am not applying specifically as a stagiaire or volunteer serving the Sanctuary in an official capacity this year, I am very open to helping in simple, informal ways whenever appropriate and permitted by the pilgrimage organizers. For example, I am comfortable assisting fellow pilgrims who might need help pushing a wheelchair over short distances, carrying bags, offering translation between English and Spanish, or simply providing companionship and prayer support to those who are sick or elderly.

If there are any optional opportunities for pilgrims to assist with non-specialized tasks—such as helping organize lines, distributing prayer leaflets, or participating in music during liturgies—I would gladly participate, as long as it does not conflict with the official program or requirements of the Sanctuary. My main intention is to be present as a prayerful and supportive pilgrim, but with a heart ready to serve quietly in the background.

Since I am applying as a regular pilgrim and not under the stagiaire program, I understand that specific assignments like St. John the Baptist Baths, Notre-Dame reception, or St. Joseph service are usually reserved for those in designated service roles and not for general pilgrims.
